team: I (Alex Ramos) built the product after five years in hospital revenue cycle management and a stint as a data scientist at a fintech startup. My co‑founder, Maya Patel, is a former software engineer at a health‑tech scale‑up; we have no dedicated sales or ops staff yet.
problem: Healthcare providers are stuck with outdated, manual collection workflows that generate high write‑offs, regulatory risk, and disgruntled patients, especially as more patients face high‑deductible plans.
solution: Our platform integrates directly with a provider’s billing system, validates claim eligibility with AI, and runs a compliant, omnichannel outreach (mail, SMS, patient portal) that automates payment plans and appeals, cutting collection time by ~30% and increasing recoveries by 12‑18%.

name: GoalRewards
description: GoalRewards turns everyday spending into a savings accelerator by attaching loyalty points to personal financial goals. Users earn points for every purchase, which automatically convert into micro‑deposits toward their chosen savings target, making the habit of saving feel like a game.
target_customer: Our primary payers are millennials and Gen Z savers who already use credit/debit cards for daily purchases and want a low‑effort way to reach specific savings milestones. They pay because the service quantifies their loyalty spend as concrete progress toward goals they care about.
pricing: We’re testing a hybrid model: a free tier that caps point‑to‑cash conversion at 5%, a premium subscription (roughly $9‑$15 /month) that lifts the cap to 10‑15% and adds partner discounts, plus a small 0.5% fee on withdrawals above $1,000. The exact price points and mix are still being validated, so the final structure may shift as we learn from early users.
traction: In the past six months we have onboarded 3,200 beta users, with 18% converting to the premium tier and an average monthly deposit increase of $42 per active user. Two regional banks have signed LOIs to embed GoalRewards in their mobile apps, and we’ve secured a $750k seed round.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel previously led product at a top‑10 neobank and holds an MSc in Financial Engineering; CTO Luis Gomez spent five years building real‑time rewards platforms for a global card network.
problem: Many consumers struggle to tie everyday discretionary spending to long‑term financial goals; loyalty programs reward brands, not the saver, so the incentive to save is weak and fragmented.
solution: GoalRewards overlays a goal‑based rewards engine on existing payment cards, automatically converting purchase points into cash contributions for user‑defined savings targets. The platform syncs with bank accounts, offers transparent tracking, and partners with merchants to boost conversion rates, turning routine spending into a purposeful savings habit.

name: SwiftSettle
description: SwiftSettle is a white‑label payment layer that lets boutique marketplace sellers receive funds the instant a buyer’s payment clears, bypassing the typical 2‑5 day hold. We hook into the marketplace's checkout API and push the seller's net amount to their linked bank or prepaid card within seconds.
target_customer: Marketplace operators (e.g., niche craft platforms, bespoke furniture sites) pay us because instant cash flow reduces seller churn and lets them reinvest in inventory faster.
pricing: We charge a flat 0.8 % of each transaction plus a $0.15 processing fee; volume‑based discounts kick in at $250k monthly volume.
traction: Beta with two micro‑marketplaces (GoldenThread Crafts & ForgeFurniture) covering 180 active sellers, average payout latency down from 72 hrs to 5 secs, and a 12 % lift in weekly sales for those sellers over the last 8 weeks.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel previously led payments ops at Square, building real‑time ACH pipelines; CTO Luis Gomez was a senior engineer on Stripe Radar and has deep experience with low‑latency banking APIs.
problem: Small‑scale marketplaces lock sellers' money for days after a sale, starving artisans and makers of cash when they need it most to restock or fulfill orders.
solution: By embedding directly into the marketplace's checkout, we pre‑authorize the buyer’s funds, settle instantly with our banking partners, and push the net amount to the seller’s account via RTP/instant ACH, all while handling compliance and fraud checks.

name: AirPulse Health
description: AirPulse Health delivers a connected, AI‑driven platform that continuously monitors lung function, activity, and environment for COPD patients at home. The data is streamed to clinicians in real time, triggering alerts before an exacerbation becomes a hospitalization.
target_customer: Our primary buyers are health systems and Medicare Advantage plans that reimburse for remote patient monitoring; they pay because it reduces costly COPD admissions and improves quality metrics.
pricing: Subscription model: $49 per patient per month for the hardware kit + software, with volume discounts for health systems (>500 patients) at $39/patient/mo. We also offer a per‑alert reimbursement option for payers.
traction: Pilot with three health systems (2 academic hospitals, 1 regional health plan) enrolling 152 COPD patients over 6 months; we observed a 27% drop in ER visits and a 15% increase in medication adherence. Two insurers have signed LOIs covering 2,000 patients in 2024, and we earned a $250k grant from the NIH for our AI risk engine.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel – former pulmonology fellow turned med‑tech entrepreneur, previously led digital health product at a $500M telehealth startup. CTO Luis Ramirez – ex‑Google Health engineer who built large‑scale sensor data pipelines for wearables.
problem: COPD exacerbations are the leading cause of emergency visits for chronic disease, costing the U.S. healthcare system $50B annually. Patients are often unaware that their symptoms are worsening until it’s too late, and clinicians lack real‑time data to intervene.
solution: AirPulse equips patients with a pocket‑sized spirometer, wearable pulse‑oximeter, and an ambient air‑quality sensor that feed into a cloud AI platform. Clinicians get a daily risk score and automated alerts when physiology or environmental triggers cross thresholds, allowing pre‑emptive treatment such as medication adjustments or home visits.

name: CampusCare
description: CampusCare is a web‑based platform that tries to streamline how college students get routed to mental‑health resources. We aim to provide a quick “triage” questionnaire and then point users toward counseling, peer support, or emergency help.
target_customer: Universities and colleges that fund student health services and want a low‑cost way to offer a digital front‑door for mental‑health referrals.
pricing: We charge a flat subscription per active student per year, roughly $2‑$4 per student depending on campus size.
traction: We ran a small pilot at one unnamed university last semester; a handful of students completed the questionnaire, but we don’t have hard numbers on usage or outcomes yet.
team: I’m the founder, a former psychology major who worked as a peer counselor and later did a brief stint in product design at a small ed‑tech startup.
problem: College campuses often have long wait times for counseling and students don’t know where to go for different levels of mental‑health help.
solution: Our platform offers a short, adaptive questionnaire that categorizes urgency and automatically sends the student to the appropriate resource—whether that’s an on‑campus therapist, a peer‑support group, or a crisis line.

name: NephroSync
description: NephroSync is a cloud‑based care‑coordination platform that links dialysis clinics, home health agencies, and insurers in a single, real‑time workflow. It automates appointment scheduling, lab result sharing, and discharge handoffs so patients spend less time stuck in administrative limbo.
target_customer: Our primary paying customers are dialysis providers (large chains and independent centers) and health plans that want to reduce avoidable hospitalizations and improve quality metrics. They pay because the platform cuts operational waste and helps them meet CMS quality incentives.
pricing: We charge a per‑patient‑per‑month subscription ranging from $30‑$50 depending on integration depth, with a modest implementation fee for data on‑boarding. Large networks receive volume discounts.
traction: We completed a 3‑month pilot with two midsize dialysis clinics (≈250 patients) that saw a 12% drop in missed treatments and a $15k/month reduction in overtime staffing. The pilots generated a $75k ARR LOI, but we are still negotiating the final contract.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, a former nephrology fellow turned health‑IT product lead (7 years at a dialysis EMR vendor). Our CTO, Luis Ramirez, built telehealth platforms for a regional hospital system and is our only other full‑time engineer.
problem: Dialysis patients need tightly coordinated care across multiple touchpoints, yet scheduling, lab reporting, and discharge instructions are still managed on fax or email, leading to missed appointments, medication errors, and costly rehospitalizations.
solution: NephroSync centralizes all stakeholder workflows in a HIPAA‑compliant dashboard, pushes automated alerts for upcoming sessions, flags abnormal labs, and provides a shared care‑plan that updates in real time for every party involved.

name: ChartEase
description: ChartEase is an AI‑powered scribing platform that sits inside a solo doctor's electronic medical record and turns spoken patient encounters into clean, billable notes in real time. The system learns from each doctor’s style, so the output feels native and requires minimal editing.
target_customer: The paying customer is the solo family‑practice physician (or the small practice that employs them). They buy because it frees up 30‑45 minutes per day of admin time, letting them see more patients and reduce burnout.
pricing: We’re currently experimenting with a hybrid model: a modest monthly subscription (≈$149 / physician) plus a per‑note fee that ranges from $0.25‑$0.45 based on note length. The exact split is still being validated with our beta users, so the final price points are not locked down yet.
traction: In the last 3 months we’ve run a closed beta with 12 solo doctors, averaging 85 % adoption of daily notes and reporting a 2‑hour weekly time savings. Two of those doctors have already agreed to a 6‑month pilot at a discounted rate, and we’ve secured a letter of intent from a regional physicians' network for a future rollout.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel is a former EMR product manager at Athenahealth and a board‑certified family physician. Cofounder & CTO Luis Romero spent 6 years building NLP pipelines for a major health‑AI startup.
problem: Solo family practitioners spend up to half their day on documentation, which cuts into patient time, drives burnout, and adds hidden costs to a practice that can’t afford a full‑time scribe.
solution: ChartEase listens to the doctor‑patient conversation, auto‑generates a structured SOAP note, and pushes it directly into the practice’s EMR. The AI learns the doctor’s voice and specialty language, delivering notes that require <5 % manual correction.

name: AuthFlow
description: AuthFlow is a SaaS platform that automates prior‑authorization (PA) workflows for small, regional health insurers. By pulling clinical data straight from EMR APIs and generating completion‑ready PA packets, we cut the average turnaround from days to minutes.
target_customer: Regional health plans and specialty carriers that pay for the service because it reduces staff overtime and improves provider satisfaction, which directly impacts their member retention rates.
pricing: We charge per‑request with a tiered volume model – $0.75 per PA up to 5,000 requests/month, then $0.60 thereafter. A flat‑fee “enterprise” add‑on (API SLA, custom integration) starts at $5,000/month.
traction: In Q4‑2025 we ran a 3‑month pilot with a 12‑state Medicaid managed care organization, processing 212 authentications with a 94% first‑pass success rate and shaving 3.2 days off average approval time. The pilot converted to a paid contract for $2,800/month.
team: Founder — Maya Patel, former data analyst at a large health‑information exchange and co‑author of an FDA‑cleared decision‑support tool; CTO — Raj Singh, ex‑engineer at a major payer’s claims platform.
problem: Small insurers still rely on faxed PDFs and manual data entry for prior authorizations, leading to delays, costly rework, and disgruntled providers.
solution: AuthFlow provides a plug‑and‑play API that extracts required clinical elements from the provider’s EMR, validates them against payer rules, and submits a complete PA packet directly to the insurer’s adjudication system, eliminating hand‑keyed data and reducing error rates.

name: Infusio
description: Infusio is a SaaS platform that coordinates home infusion therapy—scheduling nurses, managing inventory, and automating insurance billing—in real time. By linking patients, providers, and payers on a single secure dashboard we eliminate the paperwork and phone‑tag that currently stall treatment.
target_customer: Primary payers are outpatient infusion centers and health systems that contract home‑based therapy to reduce hospital readmissions; they fund the platform because it cuts administrative cost and improves reimbursement compliance.
pricing: Subscription per active patient: $49/mo for the core scheduling & inventory module, plus a $0.80 transaction fee for each insurance claim processed. Enterprise bundles for health systems start at $3,500/mo for up to 200 patients.
traction: Pilot with two major health systems (one 150‑bed hospital and one regional infusion network) covering 420 patients over six months; we achieved a 32% reduction in claim denials and a 15% rise in therapy adherence. The pilots generated $85k ARR and three letters of intent for a full‑scale rollout.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel previously led product at a $500M tele‑health firm and holds a PharmD; CTO Luis Gómez built real‑time logistics platforms for a national pharmacy chain, exiting in 2021.
problem: Home infusion therapy is plagued by fragmented communication: nurses manually track medication supplies, clinics chase insurance approvals, and patients often miss doses because they lack a single source of truth.
solution: Infusio provides a cloud‑native hub where clinicians upload orders, an algorithm schedules qualified nurses, inventory is auto‑replenished via integrated pharmacy APIs, and claims are submitted automatically. The result is a seamless end‑to‑end workflow that cuts admin time by half and keeps patients on schedule.

name: NutriBari
description: NutriBari is a mobile app that lets people who have had bariatric surgery log what they eat and get general guidance on meeting their nutritional needs. We’re building a simple dashboard that syncs with popular wearables and offers reminders to stay on track.
target_customer: The primary payer is the patient (or their insurance) because post‑op nutrition compliance is seen as a cost‑saving measure for avoiding complications.
pricing: We plan a freemium model: basic logging for free, premium features (custom meal plans, data export for clinicians) at $9‑$12 per month per user.
traction: We have a rough prototype and have spoken with a few surgeons who said the idea sounded useful, but we haven’t signed any pilots or generated revenue yet.
team: Founder Alex Rivera is a former diet‑etics intern who worked at a bariatric clinic and has a basic background in app development.
problem: Bariatric patients often struggle to meet micronutrient targets after surgery, leading to deficiencies and readmissions, and there’s no easy way for them to track intake in a bariatric‑specific way.
solution: Our app provides a simple food‑logging interface pre‑loaded with bariatric‑approved portion sizes and auto‑calculates macro‑ and micronutrient totals, sending push notifications to encourage adherence.

name: SilverEase
description: SilverEase is a SaaS platform that delivers personalized, sensor‑guided exercise programs and remote coaching for adults over 65 who suffer from chronic musculoskeletal pain. We combine wearable data, simple AI recommendations, and integration with existing EMR workflows to keep patients active and reduce reliance on medication.
target_customer: Our primary buyers are Medicare Advantage plans and integrated health systems that need to lower costly pain‑related hospitalizations and opioid prescriptions while improving quality‑of‑life metrics for their senior members.
pricing: We charge a per‑member‑per‑month license of $30–$45 (tiered by volume), plus a modest implementation fee for EMR integration. Clinics can also opt for a flat‑rate bundle of $2,500 per quarter for up to 200 active users.
traction: In the last 4 months we ran a pilot with two outpatient PT clinics and a regional health network, enrolling 120 seniors and achieving a 22% reduction in self‑reported pain scores and a 15% drop in opioid use versus baseline. We have a Letter of Intent from a Medicare Advantage plan covering 5,000 members, but we’re still early on revenue.
team: I’m a former physiotherapist and clinical researcher (PhD, NIH‑funded pain studies) who built the therapeutic content; my co‑founder is a solo full‑stack engineer with two years of SaaS experience. Apart from us we have a part‑time UX consultant and an advisory board of two clinicians.
problem: More than 30% of adults over 65 live with chronic musculoskeletal pain, yet adherence to evidence‑based exercise programs is low, and the fragmented care model drives over‑prescription of opioids and costly ER visits.
solution: SilverEase delivers a low‑friction, mobile‑first exercise app that syncs with inexpensive wearables to monitor motion, automatically adjusts difficulty, and pushes real‑time feedback to both the patient and their care team. The platform plugs into the health system’s EMR, generating compliance reports that insurers can use for value‑based reimbursement.

name: RuralDent AI
description: We’ve built an on‑device AI that reads dental X‑rays and flags cavities, periodontal disease, and early lesions in seconds. The model runs on cheap hardware so clinics without a specialist can get a second opinion right after the scan.
target_customer: The payer is the clinic owner or the regional health authority that funds primary‑care services. They need a cheap way to reduce referrals and avoid missed diagnoses that cost money and lives.
pricing: We’re still working out the optimal price – right now we’re testing three ideas: a flat monthly subscription of $150‑$250 per clinic, a per‑image fee of $0.30‑$0.70, and a volume‑based tier for health districts. None of these have been cemented yet; we’re gathering feedback on what makes sense for cash‑strapped rural practices.
traction: In the past six months we piloted the software in 5 rural dental clinics across two states. Accuracy on their test set hit 92 % vs a board‑certified radiologist, and three clinics have already reduced specialist referrals by ~30 %. We have letters of intent from two regional health systems pending a pricing decision.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, former DMD‑researcher who spent 7 years in a university oral‑radiology lab and led AI projects at a med‑tech startup. Co‑founder and CTO Luis Gomez previously built edge‑AI platforms for a telecom company and has a PhD in computer vision.
problem: Rural dental clinics often lack on‑site radiologists, so X‑rays are either sent out for review (costly and slow) or interpreted by general dentists with limited training, leading to delayed or missed diagnoses.
solution: Our AI runs on a low‑cost PC attached to the X‑ray unit, instantly scans the image, highlights suspect areas, and provides a confidence score. Clinics get a diagnostic aide at the point of care, cutting referral times and improving patient outcomes.

name: RareMatch.ai
description: RareMatch.ai is a SaaS platform that uses AI‑driven phenotypic profiling to connect patients with ultra‑rare disease diagnoses to the handful of clinical trials that actually exist for them. We automate the manual chart review and outreach that biotech sponsors currently do, cutting lead time from months to weeks.
target_customer: Pharma and biotech companies developing rare‑disease therapies – they pay because recruiting the right patients is the biggest cost and risk in these trials, and they need a reliable pipeline of qualified participants.
pricing: Subscription tier for small‑phase studies: $4,500 per month for up to 30 matched patients; enterprise tier for multi‑phase programs: $12,000 per month plus $250 per confirmed enrolment.
traction: In the past 6 months we have secured pilot agreements with two Phase II rare‑disease programs (one for Batten disease, one for Pompe disease). Both pilots have already identified 12 eligible patients each, half of whom have consented to screen. Our advisory board includes three patient‑advocacy leaders who are actively referring patients.
team: Founder Dr. Maya Patel, MD/PhD, spent 7 years in clinical genetics and led patient‑recruitment strategy at a top CRO; CTO Luis Gomez, formerly machine‑learning lead at a health‑AI startup, built the phenotypic matching engine.
problem: Rare‑disease trial sponsors spend disproportionate time and money sifting through electronic health records and contacting clinicians, yet still miss the few suitable participants, leading to delayed or failed studies.
solution: RareMatch.ai ingests de‑identified EHR data, applies a rare‑disease ontology, and scores patients against trial inclusion criteria. The platform then automates outreach to the patients’ care teams and tracks consent, giving sponsors a real‑time dashboard of ready‑to‑screen candidates.

name: RxOptics
description: RxOptics is a SaaS platform that gives self‑insured employers direct, real‑time control over their pharmacy benefit spend. Using AI to match patients to the lowest‑cost in‑network drugs and automatically negotiate rebates, we turn a black‑box PBM into a transparent cost‑saving engine.
target_customer: HR/Benefits teams at self‑insured mid‑size to large employers who fund their own drug spend and need predictable, lower costs without sacrificing employee health.
pricing: A usage‑based model – $3‑$5 per covered employee per month plus a modest 2% transaction fee on any rebate captured. Most pilots see a net‑savings of 12‑18% on pharmacy spend, making the fee easily offset.
traction: In the last 6 months we launched pilots with 4 self‑insured employers (totaling 7,800 covered lives) and have already demonstrated $1.1 M in annualized drug spend reductions, earning $220 K in captured rebates. Two of the pilots have converted to paid contracts, and a Fortune‑500 client is in negotiations for a 2027 rollout.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel spent 6 years as a senior analyst at a top‑tier PBM, building pricing models that saved clients $200 M+. CTO Luis Gomez is a former health‑data engineer at an EHR startup and led the AI‑prescription engine that now powers RxOptics.
problem: Self‑insured employers face opaque pharmacy benefit contracts, unpredictable rebates, and high admin overhead, leading to 15‑20% of drug spend slipping through without visibility or control.
solution: Our platform ingests claim data in real time, runs an optimization engine that routes each prescription to the cheapest in‑network product, negotiates dynamic rebates, and provides a dashboard that lets benefits managers see every dollar saved. The result is a transparent, controllable pharmacy benefit that lowers spend and improves employee satisfaction.

name: KidTherapy Scheduler
description: KidTherapy Scheduler is a lightweight web platform that aims to simplify how pediatric therapy appointments are booked and managed. We’re trying to let parents, clinics and therapists keep their calendars in sync without a lot of back‑and‑forth.
target_customer: The paying customers would be therapy clinics and independent pediatric therapists who need a simple way to collect fees for the scheduling service; parents indirectly benefit but don’t pay directly.
pricing: We plan to charge a modest monthly subscription per provider – roughly $100‑$200 a month depending on the number of active children, with a free trial period.
traction: We have a small pilot with a couple of local clinics that have signed up for the beta, but usage is still sporadic and we don’t yet have any paying customers or measurable retention data.
team: I’m the founder, a former pediatric physiotherapist who taught myself basic full‑stack development and am now building this with a part‑time software engineer friend.
problem: Scheduling pediatric therapy is currently fragmented – parents have to call multiple clinics, coordinate with school schedules, and deal with missed or double‑booked appointments.
solution: Our platform offers a single calendar view, automated reminders and a simple booking flow that lets parents request slots and therapists approve them, hoping to reduce the admin overhead and missed sessions.

name: OncoSecond
description: OncoSecond is a tele‑oncology platform that connects cancer patients with board‑certified oncologists for a paid, on‑demand second opinion. We use a secure video portal and a curated knowledge base to streamline case review and deliver a written report within 72 hours.
target_customer: Patients (or their families) who have already been diagnosed and are looking for confirmation or alternative treatment options; they pay out‑of‑pocket because insurers rarely cover independent second opinions for cancer.
pricing: Flat fee per case: $299 for a standard review (recorded consult + 2‑page report) and $499 for an advanced review that includes a treatment plan and medication cost‑benefit analysis.
traction: Since our soft launch three months ago we have completed 48 consultations, with a 78 % repeat‑booking rate and a Net Promoter Score of 62. We’ve also signed a provisional partnership with a regional cancer centre that will refer patients to us for a revenue‑share pilot.
team: Founder/CEO Maya Patel is a former clinical oncologist turned data‑science PhD; CTO Amir Rahman is a solo developer who built the video‑stack for a tele‑dermatology startup that never launched. We currently have no dedicated sales or regulatory staff.
problem: Many cancer patients receive a single opinion in a fragmented care system, feel uncertain about treatment choices, and struggle to get an affordable, timely second opinion that isn’t limited to their local hospital network.
solution: OncoSecond aggregates a vetted roster of oncologists across major US centres, lets patients upload imaging and pathology reports securely, and delivers a video consult plus a concise written opinion within days—cutting wait times, travel costs, and the emotional toll of uncertainty.

name: SomniWell
description: SomniWell is a mobile-first digital therapeutic that delivers evidence‑based CBT‑I, sleep‑tracking, and AI‑personalized coaching to help adults overcome chronic insomnia. We combine clinically‑validated modules with real‑time data to adjust the program on the fly, aiming to replace costly sleep meds and endless doctor visits.
target_customer: The end user is the insomnia sufferer, but the paying party is usually the individual (self‑pay) or their employer/health plan looking to reduce productivity loss and medical costs associated with poor sleep.
pricing: We’re currently piloting a subscription model. Early testers have been on a $9‑$19 per month tier, with a premium $29 option that adds live coach chats. The exact structure is still being validated, so we haven’t locked in a final price point yet.
traction: In the past 6 months we’ve run a closed beta with 2,300 users who logged over 5,000 nights of sleep data; 68% reported a ≥2‑point drop in the ISI score after 4 weeks. We’ve secured a pilot with a regional health insurer that will roll out to 5,000 members later this year.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, a former clinical psychologist who led insomnia research at a major academic hospital, and co‑founder Alex Chen, a former senior engineer at a wearable‑tech startup who built the data‑pipeline for our app.
problem: Chronic insomnia affects roughly 10% of adults, leading to reduced productivity, higher healthcare utilization, and dependence on prescription medication, yet access to consistent CBT‑I is limited and expensive.
solution: SomniWell delivers a fully digital CBT‑I program that adapts to each user’s sleep patterns via sensor data and AI, providing interactive lessons, habit‑building tools, and optional therapist‑assist sessions—all on a smartphone.

name: Maternix Pulse
description: Maternix Pulse gives obstetric teams a continuous, at‑home monitoring solution for high‑risk pregnancies—think pre‑eclampsia, gestational diabetes, and placenta previa. A lightweight wearable streams vitals to a cloud AI that flags deterioration before the patient even feels sick, letting clinicians intervene early.
target_customer: Hospital obstetrics departments and private maternal‑fetal medicine clinics pay for the service because it reduces ICU transfers, shortens length of stay, and helps meet quality metrics for high‑risk pregnancy management.
pricing: We charge per monitored patient: $150 / month for the sensor kit and platform access, plus a $30 / event fee for each clinician‑triggered alert that requires a tele‑visit. Hospital‑wide contracts start at $12K / year for up‑to‑80 patients.
traction: Pilot at a Level‑III NICU in Boston with 32 high‑risk patients showed a 22% drop in emergency transfers over 6 months and earned us a $250K NIH Small Business Innovation Research grant. Two OB‑GYN groups have signed LOIs for a 12‑month rollout.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, a former maternal‑fetal medicine fellow who spent the last two years building clinical decision tools at a health‑system analytics lab. Our CTO, Alex Huang, is a MIT PhD in biomedical signal processing who previously led sensor development at a wearable startup.
problem: High‑risk pregnancies require frequent in‑person visits, yet many adverse events (e.g., rising blood pressure, abnormal heart‑rate variability) occur between appointments, leading to preventable ER trips and maternal morbidity.
solution: Our FDA‑cleared, patch‑style sensor measures blood pressure, heart rate, respiration, and fetal HR continuously; AI algorithms detect early patterns of distress and push alerts to the care team’s EHR dashboard, enabling proactive outreach or medication adjustment before a crisis develops.

name: NavMed
description: NavMed is an AI‑powered care‑navigation platform built for Medicaid members and their managing organizations. It delivers personalized, bilingual guidance—via SMS, voice calls, and a web portal—to help members schedule primary care, complete referrals, and avoid unnecessary ER visits.
target_customer: State Medicaid agencies and their Managed Care Organizations (MCOs) pay for NavMed because it drives down cost of care while meeting quality benchmarks required by federal and state regulators.
pricing: Subscription model at $2‑$5 per member per month, tiered by enrollment volume and level of live‑coach support. Pilot contracts start at $2,000 /mo for up to 5,000 members.
traction: In Q4‑2025 we launched pilots with two state Medicaid programs covering 15,000 members. Participants showed a 30% rise in primary‑care appointment adherence and a 12% reduction in avoidable ER utilization, saving the programs an estimated $1.1 M in the first six months. Our platform currently integrates with three major Medicaid eligibility APIs and has a Net Promoter Score of 78 among enrolled members.
team: Founder Jane Doe (ex‑Director of Population Health at a large health system, MBA, former Medicaid policy advisor) and CTO John Smith (PhD in machine learning, previously led data products at a top‑10 MCO).
problem: Medicaid members often lack a single, trusted source for navigating fragmented care—leading to missed appointments, duplicate services, and costly emergency‑room visits. Providers and MCOs have no scalable way to give each member the personalized guidance needed to stay in‑network and compliant with care plans.
solution: NavMed combines real‑time eligibility data, predictive analytics, and a network of trained care coaches to deliver timely, culturally relevant nudges and live assistance. The platform automatically identifies gaps in care, reaches members through their preferred channel, and schedules appointments or arranges transportation, all while reporting measurable outcomes back to the Medicaid agency.

name: FlexiCare
description: FlexiCare is a platform that connects employees with physical therapists who specialize in musculoskeletal issues. We aim to make it easier for workplaces to offer some kind of on‑demand support for back, neck, and joint discomfort.
target_customer: HR departments and benefits managers at midsize to large companies that want to improve wellness offerings and reduce injury‑related costs.
pricing: Subscription‑based model per employee, roughly $5‑$10 per active user per month, with a small setup fee for onboarding.
traction: We have done a handful of informal pilots with a couple of local businesses, but we don’t yet have any signed contracts or measurable usage data.
team: Founder Jane Doe previously worked as a product manager at a generic SaaS startup and holds a degree in kinesiology; co‑founder John Smith is a former PT clinic administrator.
problem: Many employers struggle to address musculoskeletal pain among staff, leading to lost productivity and higher health costs, but existing solutions are either too expensive or hard to integrate.
solution: Our web‑based portal lets employees request virtual PT sessions, book in‑person visits, and access basic exercise videos, all coordinated through the employer’s HR platform.

name: DischargeFlow
description: DischargeFlow is a SaaS platform that guides hospital case managers through the entire discharge planning workflow, from eligibility checks to post‑acute follow‑up. By surfacing real‑time bed availability and automating patient‑specific checklists, we aim to cut readmission risk without adding paperwork.
target_customer: Our primary buyers are large hospital systems and health networks that own the case‑management and utilization‑review departments; they pay because reduced readmissions translate directly into penalty avoidance and higher CMS reimbursements.
pricing: We charge a per‑bed‑month license ranging from $1.20‑$2.00 per staffed inpatient bed, plus a modest implementation fee ($5‑10k) for integration with the EHR. Volume discounts kick in after 1,000 beds.
traction: In the past six months we piloted with two regional health systems (total 800 beds) and saw a 12% drop in 30‑day readmissions on the pilot sites. We have 15 active users and are in talks with three additional hospitals for a Q3 rollout, but adoption is still modest compared with incumbents.
team: I’m a former case‑manager turned product lead (10 years in hospital operations) and my co‑founder is a full‑stack engineer who spent three years building decision‑support tools at a larger health‑IT vendor. No dedicated sales or ops staff yet.
problem: Hospitals lose $30‑$50 billion annually to preventable readmissions, largely because discharge planning is fragmented across paper checklists, siloed EHR modules, and delayed communication with post‑acute providers.
solution: DischargeFlow consolidates eligibility rules, bed‑availability data, and patient‑specific discharge tasks into a single, mobile‑first interface that pushes real‑time alerts to case managers and automatically logs compliance for audit, making it easier to close the loop before the patient leaves.

name: SeptoSense
description: SeptoSense leverages continuous physiologic data streams from ICU monitors to run a proprietary, AI‑driven early‑warning algorithm for sepsis. The system flags at‑risk patients in real time and delivers actionable alerts directly to clinicians' dashboards.
target_customer: Hospital systems and ICU directors who pay for technology that can reduce sepsis mortality and costly ICU stays; the value proposition is lower mortality rates and shorter lengths of stay, which translate into reimbursement bonuses and lower litigation risk.
pricing: We’re still iterating on the pricing model. Currently we’re piloting a per‑bed‑month subscription (estimated $150–$250 per ICU bed) and a usage‑based add‑on for each alert that triggers a full clinical response. The exact mix will depend on hospital size and the level of integration required.
traction: In the past 6 months we’ve completed a 3‑site pilot covering 120 ICU beds, achieving a 2.1× reduction in time‑to‑antibiotics and an 18% drop in sepsis‑related LOS. Two of the pilot sites have signed letters of intent for a full rollout next year.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, former ICU fellow turned data scientist, and my co‑founder Alex Liu is a veteran software engineer from a major EHR vendor. Together we’ve built and scaled clinical decision‑support tools in three previous health‑tech startups.
problem: Sepsis kills 1 in 3 ICU patients, and current detection relies on intermittent lab results and subjective assessments, leading to delayed treatment and higher mortality.
solution: Our platform ingests high‑frequency vitals, lab feeds, and medication data to compute a sepsis risk score every 30 seconds, surfacing high‑confidence alerts to the care team before clinical deterioration becomes obvious.

name: DermRural AI
description: DermRural AI is a low‑bandwidth tele‑dermatology platform that lets primary‑care nurses in remote clinics upload photos of skin lesions and receive board‑certified dermatologist reads within 24 hours. We combine a simple mobile app with an AI‑triage engine to prioritize urgent cases and keep referral costs down.
target_customer: County health departments and NGOs that fund rural primary‑care clinics; they pay because the service replaces costly trips to city hospitals and reduces missed skin‑cancer diagnoses among underserved populations.
pricing: Subscription per clinic: $120/month for up to 30 cases, plus $5 per additional case. A “pay‑as‑you‑go” option at $8 per case for sporadic users. Discounts for government contracts (>20 clinics).
traction: Pilot with three PHC centers in West Virginia processed 212 lesions in the first 8 weeks, achieving a 92 % clinician satisfaction score and identifying two basal‑cell carcinomas that would have been missed. We have a 4‑clinic LOI from a state agency for the next year.
team: Founder Dr. Maya Patel, MD (dermatology residency, 3 years as tele‑derm consultant) and ex‑Navy medical logistics officer who built a secure data pipeline for field hospitals; CTO Rahul Singh, former ML engineer at a health‑AI startup.
problem: Residents of rural counties travel an average of 80 miles for a dermatologist visit; delays lead to late skin‑cancer detection and higher treatment costs, while local nurses lack specialist support to triage lesions.
solution: A lightweight Android app that works on 3G networks lets nurses capture, anonymize, and send images to our specialist pool. An AI model pre‑screens images for urgency, routing high‑risk cases for same‑day dermatologist review, and delivers a concise diagnosis and care plan back to the clinic.

name: CareShift
description: CareShift is a real‑time marketplace that connects per‑diem nurses with senior‑living communities that need shift coverage. Facilities post open shifts, vetted nurses accept them through a mobile app, and the platform handles compliance paperwork and payment.
target_customer: The paying customers are senior‑living operators and their care managers, who need a reliable, cost‑effective way to keep every resident covered without resorting to costly agency contracts.
pricing: We charge a transaction fee of 12% of the nurse’s hourly rate (capped at $35/shift) plus a optional $1,500/month subscription for advanced analytics and priority placement.
traction: In the last 6 months we ran pilots with 12 independent senior‑living communities, covering 860 shifts and generating $115K ARR. Fill rate is 87% and average time‑to‑fill dropped from 48 hrs (industry average) to 6 hrs. Two of the pilots have signed three‑year contracts, and we have a waitlist of 5 additional facilities.
team: Founder Emma Liu is a former RN manager for a 200‑bed assisted‑living chain and led a $3M staffing initiative; CTO Ravi Patel previously built a gig‑economy platform at a Series‑C startup.
problem: Senior‑living operators face chronic nurse shortages, high turnover, and expensive agency fees, which lead to understaffed floors, compliance risks, and resident dissatisfaction.
solution: Our platform aggregates a vetted pool of per‑diem nurses, matches them to open shifts via AI‑driven scheduling, automates credential verification, and streams payments, giving facilities a single, predictable source for on‑demand staffing.

name: ClinicShift
description: ClinicShift is a lightweight service that helps small, independent clinics move their patient data from one EHR system to another. We aim to make the migration process less painful and more predictable for practices that don’t have big IT teams.
target_customer: The paying customers are the clinic owners or office managers who are responsible for the EHR budget and want to avoid costly downtime during a switch.
pricing: We charge a flat fee per migration project (roughly $2,500‑$5,000 depending on data volume) plus an optional monthly support add‑on of about $200.
traction: So far we’ve had informal conversations with about a dozen clinics and two of them signed letters of intent, but we haven’t closed any paying contracts yet.
team: I’m the founder, a former practice manager who spent five years overseeing EHR roll‑outs at a regional health network, and I’ve been building this side‑project part‑time.
problem: Small clinics often have to switch EHR vendors because of pricing or feature gaps, but the migration process is technically complex, time‑consuming, and risky for patient safety.
solution: ClinicShift provides a guided migration workflow and a set of scripts that extract, transform, and load patient records, plus a project manager who coordinates with both the old and new vendors to keep the clinic running.

name: ValorMind
description: ValorMind is a mobile‑first platform that delivers evidence‑based therapy, peer‑to‑peer support, and benefits navigation specifically for U.S. military veterans coping with PTSD, depression, and transition stress. We bundle CBT lessons, guided meditation, and a moderated community into a single, VA‑compatible app.
target_customer: Our paying customers are VA health systems and private insurers that fund mental‑health care for veterans; they buy the platform to boost engagement, lower crisis‑intervention costs, and meet mandated outcomes for veteran wellness.
pricing: Enterprise license starts at $10,000 per year for up to 1,000 active users, with a $12 / user / month tier for smaller insurers; we also offer a limited free‑tier for individual veterans to drive adoption.
traction: We ran a 3‑month pilot with two VA clinics (≈300 active users) and saw weekly engagement stay above 40% and a 15% average drop in self‑reported PHQ‑9 scores. The pilot generated a letter of intent from the regional VA for a 12‑month rollout, but we’re still competing with several larger mental‑health platforms that have already entered the veteran space.
team: I’m a combat‑veteran and former DoD psychologist (10 years) who built the clinical curriculum; my co‑founder is a full‑stack engineer who previously launched a telehealth startup; we currently operate with a part‑time therapist consultant and no dedicated sales or ops staff.
problem: Veterans often struggle to find mental‑health care that is both evidence‑based and culturally attuned, face long wait times, and have to navigate a fragmented benefits system, leading to low treatment adherence and higher crisis rates.
solution: ValorMind bundles clinically validated CBT modules, on‑demand video sessions, and an AI‑driven benefits navigator into one app, while a moderated peer community reduces stigma and improves daily engagement. Integration with VA EHR APIs lets clinicians track progress and coordinate care without adding workflow friction.

name: Mindsight Care
description: Mindsight Care is a web‑and‑mobile platform that lets families of people living with dementia coordinate daily care, share real‑time updates, and access vetted professional support. Our AI‑driven dashboard surfaces warning signs and suggests activities to keep the person engaged, while a community hub connects caregivers to peer advice and licensed therapists.
target_customer: The primary payer is the adult‑children of the person with dementia, who buy a subscription to simplify coordination and reduce the emotional toll; we also see secondary interest from Medicare Advantage plans that want to lower institutional readmissions.
pricing: We currently offer a freemium tier with core scheduling and messaging, and a paid “Complete Care” tier that bundles AI insights, therapist video sessions, and priority support. The paid tier is billed monthly, but we haven’t nailed down the exact price point yet – we’re experimenting with a range from $29 to $99 per month depending on the level of professional services bundled.
traction: In the past six months we’ve run a closed‑beta with 120 families (roughly 190 individual caregivers) and see a 92% weekly active usage rate. Caregivers report a 30% reduction in time spent on coordination tasks. We have two pilot agreements with regional home‑care agencies and are in talks with a Medicare Advantage partner for a larger rollout.
team: I’m Julia Chen, former RN manager of a memory‑care unit who saw the coordination nightmare first‑hand; our CTO, Marco Alvarez, spent five years building scalable SaaS platforms for tele‑health startups.
problem: Families caring for a loved one with dementia spend hours each week juggling medication schedules, appointments, and communication with multiple providers, leading to burnout and higher risk of emergency hospitalizations.
solution: Mindsight Care centralizes all care‑related data in one secure place, automates reminders, surfaces AI‑driven risk alerts, and links families to on‑demand professional support, turning a fragmented, stressful process into a coordinated, manageable workflow.

name: SoundGuard
description: SoundGuard is a cloud‑based hearing‑health platform built for aging workers in noisy industries. We combine a quick, smartphone‑enabled screening with AI‑driven risk alerts and personalized hearing‑protection recommendations, all integrated into a company’s existing occupational‑health workflow.
target_customer: Our paying customers are mid‑size manufacturers and logistics firms with a workforce aged 45+. They fund the service to reduce safety incidents, lower workers’ compensation costs, and meet OSHA/ERISA health‑benefit obligations.
pricing: We charge a per‑employee subscription: $12 USD per active user per month, with a minimum of 100 users per contract. Optional on‑site audiology visits are billed $75 USD each.
traction: In Q4 2025 we ran a pilot with two manufacturers (total 210 workers). 87% completed the baseline screen, and the companies reported a 15% drop in reported hearing‑related near‑misses within three months. We have LOIs for three additional plants totaling ~600 users.
team: Founder & CEO Jane Doe is a licensed audiologist who spent 8 years running occupational‑health programs for a major steel producer. CTO Mark Liu is a former Amazon engineer who led AI‑based health‑monitoring products.
problem: Age‑related hearing loss is a silent safety hazard in noisy work environments. Employers struggle to identify early loss, comply with regulations, and provide cost‑effective hearing protection for older staff.
solution: SoundGuard delivers a low‑friction screening app, continuously monitors ambient noise exposure, and generates individualized protection plans that sync with a company’s PPE inventory. Managers get real‑time alerts when a worker’s risk exceeds a preset threshold, enabling proactive interventions before accidents occur.

name: EquiScreen
description: EquiScreen is a SaaS platform that embeds brief, evidence‑based social determinants of health (SDoH) screens into Medicaid member workflows and automatically routes actionable referrals to community resources. The system pulls real‑time data from local service providers to keep care teams informed of what support is actually available.
target_customer: State Medicaid agencies and Medicaid Managed Care Organizations (MCOs) – they fund the platform because it helps them meet CMS quality metrics, reduce avoidable costs, and demonstrate value‑based care compliance.
pricing: Subscription model: $0.40 per screened member per month (tiered discounts after 50k members) plus an optional $0.15 per referral transaction for high‑volume plans.
traction: Running pilots with the Arizona Medicaid agency and a Mid‑Atlantic MCO covering 12,400 members; completed 8,900 screens in the first 3 months with a 68% completion rate. Early outcomes show a 12% drop in preventable ER visits among screened members and a $250k cost‑avoidance claim. Secured a $500k state innovation grant and a Letter of Intent from a third state to roll out to 50k members next quarter.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel — MPH, former Medicaid policy analyst at the CDC and product lead for a population‑health analytics startup. CTO Luis Romero — ex‑engineer at Epic and built health‑data integration pipelines for two large health systems.
problem: Medicaid programs are required to assess members’ social needs, but existing surveys are lengthy, siloed, and never translate into concrete referrals, leading to poor data quality, wasted staff time, and missed opportunities to intervene on drivers of health inequity.
solution: EquiScreen delivers a 3‑question, tablet‑compatible screen that pops up at the point of care, auto‑scores risk, and instantly pushes the member’s needs to a curated community‑resource marketplace. Care managers get real‑time dashboards, and the platform reports aggregate SDoH metrics back to the state for compliance and budgeting.

name: MindFlow
description: MindFlow is a cloud‑based platform that tries to streamline behavioral health intake by digitizing questionnaires and pre‑visit paperwork. We aim to let clinicians focus on care rather than admin, and give patients a smoother onboarding experience.
target_customer: We sell to outpatient behavioral health clinics and private practices that need to reduce front‑desk workload and improve patient engagement; the decision makers are usually office managers or clinic directors looking to cut operational costs.
pricing: A simple SaaS model: $99 per month for up to 5 clinicians, then $20 per additional clinician. We also offer a small custom‑integration fee for larger organizations.
traction: We have a clickable demo and a handful of informal beta testers (two therapists who gave us vague positive feedback). No paying customers yet, and we are still iterating on the core questionnaire engine.
team: Founder Alex Rivera has a background in software engineering and spent three years as a product manager at a mental‑health telehealth startup. The co‑founder, Priya Patel, is a licensed clinical social worker who has worked in community clinics.
problem: Behavioral health providers waste a lot of time on manual intake forms, phone triage, and re‑entering patient data, which leads to delays and missed appointments.
solution: MindFlow offers an AI‑assisted, web‑based intake flow that patients can complete before their first visit; the data is automatically normalized and pushed into the clinic’s EMR, reducing manual entry and speeding up the scheduling process.

name: TheraNote
description: TheraNote is a SaaS platform that uses AI to turn a physical therapist's spoken session notes into compliant, billable documentation in seconds. We integrate with the major EMR systems used in outpatient PT clinics to auto‑populate treatment plans and progress notes.
target_customer: Clinic owners and practice managers pay for the service because it saves therapist time, reduces claim denials, and improves documentation compliance.
pricing: Subscription per therapist: $29/month for the basic auto‑transcription tier; $49/month for the full EMR‑sync + analytics tier. Enterprise contracts get volume discounts.
traction: In the past 3 months we have 12 paying clinics (≈180 therapists) generating $3.5k MRR, and a 30% reduction in average note‑taking time reported in our pilot. However, churn is a concern and competitor products are quickly adding similar AI features.
team: I am a former PT (5 yrs in outpatient practice) turned software engineer (M.S. Computer Science, 2 yrs at a health‑IT startup). My co‑founder is a data scientist with a PhD in NLP who previously built a clinical language model for a large EHR vendor.
problem: Physical therapists spend up to 25% of their workday documenting sessions, leading to fatigue, billing errors, and lost patient time. Existing EMRs are clunky and require manual entry.
solution: TheraNote captures therapists’ voice recordings at the end of a session, runs an AI model fine‑tuned on PT terminology, and pushes structured notes directly into the clinic’s EMR, cutting documentation time to under a minute while ensuring HIPAA‑compliant records.

name: MomsMind
description: We built a mobile app that delivers clinically validated postpartum depression screenings to new mothers during the first 12 weeks after birth, and instantly connects those at risk with tele‑therapy or local providers.
target_customer: Hospital systems, OB‑GYN clinics, and employer‑sponsored health plans – they pay because early detection cuts costly hospital readmissions and improves employee wellbeing.
pricing: We’re still figuring out the optimal model. Right now we’re testing a hybrid approach: $9‑$12 per screening for clinics plus an optional $1.99‑$2.99/month subscription for direct‑to‑consumer users. The final price points haven’t been locked in yet.
traction: Beta with two regional hospital networks: 3,200 screens in the last 3 months, 78% completion rate, and a 30% higher detection rate than standard postpartum follow‑up. Early feedback from clinicians is positive, but we haven’t scaled beyond the pilot.
team: I’m a former OB‑GYN resident who transitioned to software engineering to solve gaps in maternal care; my co‑founder is a Stanford‑trained data scientist with experience building FDA‑cleared digital health tools.
problem: Up to half of postpartum depression cases go undiagnosed because screening is inconsistent, stigma prevents help‑seeking, and many new mothers lack easy access to mental‑health resources.
solution: MomsMind pushes the EPDS questionnaire at evidence‑based intervals, uses AI to prioritize risk scores, and automatically routes high‑risk users to tele‑therapy or local clinicians, making screening seamless and actionable.

name: RecoverTrack
description: RecoverTrack is a lightweight, HIPAA‑compliant mobile platform that lets medication‑assisted treatment (MAT) clinics track daily opioid use‑recovery metrics for each patient and intervene before a relapse. It combines a simple self‑report UI with passive wear‑able data to give clinicians a real‑time compliance dashboard.
target_customer: The paying customers are small‑to‑mid‑size MAT clinics and sober‑living homes that need objective adherence data for Medicaid billing and state reporting; they buy because the platform reduces manual charting and improves outcomes that insurers reward.
pricing: Subscription per active patient – $30 / patient / month (capped at 200 patients) or a flat $2,500 / clinic / month for unlimited users. One‑time onboarding fee of $1,200 for integration with the clinic’s EMR.
traction: Pilot launched 6 months ago with three independent MAT clinics (total 152 patients). 78 % of patients completed daily logs for >90 days and clinic staff reported a 15 % drop in missed doses. Two clinics have renewed for a second quarter; we’re in talks for a modest Medicaid pilot.
team: Founder Dr. Maya Patel, MD, MPH – former addiction medicine fellow who built a digital adherence tool at a university hospital. CTO Alex Rivera, ex‑engineer at a wearable‑health startup, leads the mobile & data stack.
problem: MAT providers rely on self‑report and sporadic urine screens, which are costly, subject to recall bias, and don’t give clinicians timely warning of relapse, leading to higher drop‑out rates and lost reimbursement.
solution: RecoverTrack gives patients a 1‑tap daily check‑in and passive motion/sleep data from a low‑cost wrist band. Clinicians see a live compliance score, automated alerts for missed check‑ins, and can export reports for payer audits, closing the data gap without added paperwork.

name: OncoComply
description: OncoComply is an AI‑driven compliance platform that monitors oncology billing in real time, flags coding errors and automates documentation to keep practices audit‑ready. It integrates with existing EMR/EHR and payer portals, delivering actionable insights without disrupting clinicians.
target_customer: Oncology clinics and hospital oncology departments – they pay because they bear the financial risk of audits, denials and compliance penalties and need a scalable way to protect revenue.
pricing: Subscription per full‑time equivalent provider: $299/mo for practices <5 providers, $799/mo for 5‑20 providers, and custom enterprise pricing for larger networks.
traction: Pilot with 4 mid‑size cancer centers reduced claim denials by 27% and reclaimed $1.2 M in six months; secured a $500 k ARR Letter of Intent from a regional health system; accepted into the 2024 HealthTech Launchpad accelerator.
team: Founder Dr. Maya Patel, former radiation oncologist turned health data scientist who led billing analytics at a $2 B oncology network; CTO Alex Chen, ex‑Google AI engineer with 8 years building NLP pipelines for healthcare.
problem: Oncology billing is the most audit‑prone specialty – roughly 30% of claims are rejected due to coding mismatches, causing revenue loss, compliance risk and costly manual re‑work, while existing tools are generic and require extensive chart review.
solution: OncoComply deploys a proprietary NLP engine that reads clinic notes, maps them to ICD‑10/CPT codes, cross‑checks against payer rules, and surfaces errors instantly. Integrated directly into the clinician’s EHR, it enables corrections before submission, cutting denials and audit exposure.

name: RareRx Connect
description: We are building a web‑based hub that tries to bring together doctors, specialty pharmacies and payers for patients with ultra‑rare diseases. The idea is to make the ordering and reimbursement process a little less painful for everyone involved.
target_customer: Our primary buyers would be health plans and pharmacy benefit managers who want to lower administrative overhead, with secondary interest from pharma companies that sponsor rare‑disease therapies.
pricing: We are thinking of a modest monthly software fee (roughly $500‑$1,000 per plan) plus a small per‑transaction charge when a prescription is filled, but the exact model is still being worked out.
traction: We have a clickable prototype and have spoken with a handful of clinicians and one payer about the concept; no paying users or revenue yet.
team: I’m the founder, a former clinical pharmacist who spent five years in a specialty pharmacy network and saw the coordination headaches first‑hand.
problem: Patients with rare diseases often need specialty drugs that have to be ordered, approved and delivered across multiple entities, leading to delays, paperwork errors and high admin costs.
solution: Our platform would act as a single point of contact, automating prior‑authorizations, tracking shipments and providing a shared dashboard for providers, pharmacies and payers to reduce friction.

name: CardioBridge
description: CardioBridge offers a fully remote cardiac rehabilitation platform that combines wearable ECG monitoring, guided exercise videos, and weekly tele‑coach check‑ins. We aim to let post‑procedure patients complete the FDA‑recommended 12‑week program from home while staying compliant with their physicians.
target_customer: Our primary payer is health insurers and employer health plans that want to reduce readmission costs; the end users are post‑MI or post‑CABG patients who need a structured rehab program but lack access to in‑person centers.
pricing: We bill a per‑member‑per‑month (PMPM) fee of $45‑$60, covering the wearable device, software access, and tele‑coach support. Large health systems can negotiate volume discounts down to $35 PMPM.
traction: In the last six months we have enrolled 180 patients across two regional hospitals and achieved a 78% program completion rate, which is comparable to in‑person rehab. We’re in pilot talks with three additional health systems but have not yet secured a commercial contract.
team: I’m a former cardiology fellow turned product lead at a tele‑health startup; my co‑founder is a software engineer with two years at a digital health SaaS company. We have no dedicated sales or clinical staff yet, relying on advisors for those functions.
problem: Cardiac rehab participation is under 30% in the U.S. because many patients can’t travel to daily center visits, leading to higher readmission rates and higher costs for payers.
solution: By delivering FDA‑cleared ECG wearables and a curated rehab curriculum through a mobile app, we let patients perform supervised exercise at home, with data streamed to clinicians for real‑time adjustments and compliance tracking.

name: CarePath AI
description: CarePath AI is a web‑and‑mobile platform that guides patients through personalized advance care planning (ACP) conversations, captures legally‑valid directives, and syncs them directly into the health system’s EHR. We combine AI‑driven question flows with clinician dashboards to make ACP a routine part of outpatient visits.
target_customer: Hospitals and large physician groups pay for the platform because it improves quality metrics, reduces liability risk, and unlocks value‑based care incentives; they in turn offer the tool to their patients at no out‑of‑pocket cost.
pricing: We’re testing a hybrid model: an annual enterprise licence per 1,000 active patients (roughly $12‑$18k) plus an optional per‑directive fee ($5‑$10). The exact tiers are still being refined based on pilot feedback, so we don’t have a final price‑sheet yet.
traction: Beta launched in Q4 2023 with two regional health systems (≈3,200 patients enrolled, 68% completion of an advance directive within 30 days). Both partners report a 30% drop in uncoded ACP notes and have expressed interest in scaling to their entire networks.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, former chief resident in internal medicine and co‑founder of a tele‑triage startup that was acquired in 2021; my co‑founder, Alex Chen, built analytics platforms for a major EHR vendor and leads product and engineering.
problem: Only about 30% of U.S. adults have a documented advance care plan, and clinicians spend precious time navigating fragmented paper forms, leading to missed conversations and legal uncertainty.
solution: Our platform delivers a guided, conversational ACP experience that adapts to the patient’s health status, auto‑generates legally‑binding documents, and pushes them securely into the EHR, letting clinicians focus on care rather than paperwork.

name: PulmoSync
description: PulmoSync is a connected inhaler platform that streams real‑time usage data to the physician’s dashboard and to a mobile app for patients and caregivers. The hardware is a low‑cost Bluetooth add‑on that plugs into any standard metered‑dose inhaler.
target_customer: Pediatric pulmonology clinics and specialty asthma practices – they pay because the data lets them demonstrate better outcomes to insurers and justify higher reimbursement rates.
pricing: We charge the clinic a $49/month device‑management fee plus $5 per active patient per month; a typical 30‑patient practice pays around $200/month.
traction: In Q4 2025 we completed a 12‑week pilot with the Children’s Hospital of Denver, enrolling 28 children; adherence rose 22 % and clinicians reported a 15 % reduction in unscheduled visits. We have a LOI for a second site.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel is a former respiratory therapist who spent 7 years managing asthma programs in school districts; CTO Alex Kim is a software engineer with a PhD in embedded systems and two exits in medical IoT.
problem: Asthma patients miss up to 50 % of their prescribed inhaler doses, and clinicians have no reliable way to know when or how an inhaler is being used outside the office.
solution: Our plug‑and‑play Bluetooth sensor records each actuation, timestamps it, and pushes the data to a HIPAA‑compliant cloud. Physicians get a daily adherence heat map and automated alerts for missed doses, while families receive nudges and education through a simple mobile app.

name: RadAIze
description: RadAIze is an AI‑driven workflow assistant that sits between the PACS and the radiologist’s reporting station. It triages studies, auto‑populates key findings and routes cases to the right subspecialist, letting radiologists focus on interpretation rather than paperwork.
target_customer: We sell to hospital and imaging‑center radiology departments – the chief radiology officers and department administrators who need to shrink turnaround times, cut costs, and meet quality‑metric targets.
pricing: Subscription‑based per workstation: $399/mo for the Core plan (basic triage + PACS integration), $799/mo for Pro (auto‑reporting + analytics), and custom Enterprise pricing for >200 seats and on‑prem deployment.
traction: In the past 6 months we’ve run pilots at three academic hospitals (Mayo Clinic, UCSF, and NYU Langone) covering 150 radiologists; average report turnaround dropped 30% and reading errors fell 12%. We’ve signed LOIs for $1.2 M ARR from five additional health systems and are in talks with a national teleradiology network.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, an interventional radiologist turned AI researcher who led imaging analytics at a med‑tech unicorn. Our CTO, Dr. Luis Ortega, holds a PhD in computer vision and previously built large‑scale deep‑learning pipelines at Google Health.
problem: Radiology departments are drowning in volume: studies sit in PACS for hours, manual triage is error‑prone, and radiologists spend up to 20% of their time on clerical work, driving backlogs and burnout.
solution: RadAIze plugs into the existing RIS/PACS, uses a trained deep‑learning model to prioritize urgent cases, auto‑extracts structured findings, and suggests subspecialty routing. The UI integrates seamlessly into the radiologist’s workstation, delivering a pre‑populated draft that can be edited in seconds, cutting turnaround and improving consistency.

name: MindPath
description: MindPath is a mobile app that helps employees locate mental‑health resources within their company and the broader community. We aim to make the process of finding counseling, peer‑support groups, and wellness programs a little less confusing.
target_customer: HR departments and employee‑benefits managers buy the service because they want to show they care about mental health and potentially lower absenteeism.
pricing: We charge a per‑employee subscription, roughly $4–$6 per user per month, with a minimum of 50 seats.
traction: We have a beta with a couple of small startups that are testing the app, but we have no paying customers yet and limited usage data.
team: I’m the founder, a former HR analyst who spent five years at a tech company handling employee engagement surveys.
problem: Employees often don’t know what mental‑health options are available through their employer, leading to under‑utilization of benefits and higher stress.
solution: Our app aggregates internal benefit information, external provider directories, and a simple chat‑bot to guide users to the right resource, all in one searchable interface.

name: CareBridge Health
description: CareBridge Health is a tablet‑based care‑coordination platform that lets uninsured patients with chronic diseases log meds, vitals and receive remote nurse check‑ins. We embed free community‑resource directories and automate referrals to safety‑net clinics, aiming to keep patients out of the ER.
target_customer: Our paying customers are community health centers and safety‑net clinics that receive reimbursements from state Medicaid waivers; they fund us because our platform reduces costly emergency visits and improves care compliance for the uninsured they serve.
pricing: We charge a flat per‑clinic subscription of $150 / month (with a $500 setup fee) and a $25 / month add‑on for each additional nurse‑navigator seat. The patient‑facing app is free.
traction: We piloted with two Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs) serving 200 uninsured patients; 78 % of users logged at least one vitals entry per week and the clinics reported a 12 % drop in ER referrals over three months. However, we have no revenue yet and the pilots are still in a data‑collection phase.
team: I’m a former epidemiologist at the CDC who spent five years designing chronic‑disease outreach programs; I co‑found the company with a single full‑stack engineer who previously built telehealth tools at a startup. We have a part‑time medical advisor but no dedicated product or sales hires.
problem: Uninsured patients with diabetes, hypertension, or COPD often lack a consistent way to track health data and get timely clinical guidance, leading to preventable exacerbations and costly emergency care.
solution: Our platform gives patients a low‑cost tablet pre‑loaded with simple tracking tools, automated alerts for out‑of‑range readings, and scheduled video or phone check‑ins with nurses. The data syncs to the clinic’s EHR, enabling care teams to intervene early and coordinate free community resources.

name: CareBridge AI
description: CareBridge AI is a SaaS platform that surface‑to‑action care gaps for Medicare Advantage (MA) plans in real time. By blending claims analytics, electronic health record data, and a simple outreach workflow, we give health plans the tools to close gaps before they turn into costly hospitalizations.
target_customer: We sell directly to MA plan administrators (the insurers) because they own the risk and the budget for quality‑adjusted payments. Closing gaps improves their HEDIS scores and reduces spend, so they have a clear incentive to fund a solution that proves ROI.
pricing: We’re still fine‑tuning the model, but the idea is a per‑member‑per‑month (PMPM) fee that scales with the volume of members whose gaps we actively manage. Early pilots have used $1‑$3 PMPM, with discounts for larger contracts; the exact tiering is still being worked out as we gather more usage data.
traction: In Q4‑2023 we ran a 6‑month pilot with two regional MA plans covering ~5,200 lives. We identified 18% more high‑risk gaps than the plans’ existing analytics and helped reduce all‑cause readmissions by 12% in the pilot cohort. Both partners have expressed interest in expanding to the full population.
team: I’m Dr. Maya Patel, former lead data scientist at UnitedHealth’s Value-Based Care group and a PhD in Biomedical Informatics. Co‑founder Alex Romero spent five years building care‑coordination tools at a health‑tech startup that was acquired last year.
problem: MA plans struggle to detect and intervene on care gaps—missed vaccinations, unmanaged chronic conditions, medication non‑adherence—because data are siloed and actionable insights come too late, leading to higher HEDIS penalties and avoidable medical costs.
solution: Our platform ingests claims, pharmacy, and EHR data, runs predictive models to flag members at risk for specific gaps, and pushes prioritized alerts into the plan’s existing care‑management workflow. Care teams can then reach out with targeted, evidence‑based interventions directly from the dashboard.

name: OperiSync
description: OperiSync is a lightweight SaaS platform that ties together scheduling, instrument inventory, and compliance checklists for small, independent ambulatory surgery centers. It replaces dozens of spreadsheet tabs and phone calls with a single, real‑time dashboard that staff can access from any device.
target_customer: The paying customers are the owners or administrators of single‑location ASCs who need to keep operating costs low and meet state reporting requirements without hiring a full‑time operations director.
pricing: Monthly subscription: $2,000 for up to 3 operating rooms, $500 per additional OR, plus a one‑time $3,000 implementation fee for data migration and staff onboarding.
traction: In the last 6 months we ran a beta with three ASCs (total 8 ORs) that reported a 15% reduction in turnover time and a 20% drop in inventory write‑offs. Two of those centers have signed paid contracts; the third is evaluating renewal.
team: Founder Dr. Maya Patel – former operating room manager at a 150‑bed hospital and software engineer at MedTech Solutions – built the first prototype while completing an MBA focused on health‑care operations.
problem: Independent surgery centers still rely on manual spreadsheets and paper checklists to schedule cases, track instrument sets, and stay compliant with state reporting, leading to wasted staff hours, delayed case starts, and costly compliance penalties.
solution: OperiSync centralizes all of those workflows in a cloud‑native app: automated case scheduling, real‑time instrument tracking, and built‑in compliance alerts that sync with the center’s EMR, cutting admin overhead and improving day‑to‑day efficiency.

name: TrialReach
description: TrialReach is a mobile‑first platform that brings FDA‑compliant clinical trial participation to patients in rural and underserved areas, using secure video visits, on‑device biometrics, and a logistics network for home sample collection.
target_customer: Pharma sponsors and CROs pay for trial enrollment and data collection because TrialReach dramatically expands their reachable patient pool while cutting site‑setup costs.
pricing: We charge sponsors a per‑patient enrollment fee ($1,200‑$1,800 depending on study complexity) plus a flat monthly platform fee ($5k‑$10k) for data integration and logistics support.
traction: In the last 6 months we have run three pilot studies (Phase II diabetes, hypertension, and rare‑disease gene therapy) enrolling 214 participants across 12 counties, achieving 96% protocol compliance and a 40% faster enrollment compared to traditional sites. Two mid‑stage pharma companies have signed letters of intent for Q4 2026 roll‑outs.
team: Founder Dr. Maya Patel is a former FDA reviewer and clinical‑trial epidemiologist; Co‑founder Alex Gomez led product engineering at a tele‑health startup, scaling to 2M users.
problem: Rural patients are systematically excluded from clinical trials due to travel distance, lack of nearby research sites, and logistical hurdles, leading to delayed drug development and data that isn’t representative of the broader population.
solution: TrialReach equips sponsors with a SaaS platform that remotely screens, consents, and monitors participants via a HIPAA‑secure app, ships collection kits to patients’ homes, and streams real‑time data back to the sponsor, eliminating the need for physical site visits while preserving data integrity.

name: MediFlow
description: MediFlow is a cloud‑based tool that aims to make hospital supply chains a little smoother. We pull together data from different vendors and try to give managers a clearer view of what they need, when they need it.
target_customer: Our primary buyer is the hospital procurement or supply‑chain department, because they foot the bill for inventory management software and want to avoid stock‑outs or over‑ordering.
pricing: We plan to charge a simple SaaS subscription – roughly $2,000–$5,000 per month depending on hospital size and feature set.
traction: To date we’ve run a handful of informal demos with a couple of regional hospitals and have received some verbal interest, but we have no signed pilots or revenue yet.
team: I’m the founder, a former operations analyst at a large health system who saw the day‑to‑day chaos of supply ordering; my co‑founder is a software engineer with experience in data integration.
problem: Hospitals often struggle with fragmented ordering processes, leading to excess inventory, stock‑outs, and wasted staff time.
solution: MediFlow aggregates order data, predicts usage trends, and presents a basic dashboard so procurement teams can make quicker, more informed ordering decisions.

name: PulseLoop
description: PulseLoop is a lightweight SaaS platform that captures real‑time patient feedback at the point of care for ambulatory clinics. It plugs into existing EHRs and delivers a dashboard of actionable insights within hours of a visit.
target_customer: We sell to outpatient clinics and health‑system ambulatory divisions that need to improve patient satisfaction scores and meet CMS quality reporting requirements.
pricing: Subscription per provider – $99/mo for up to 5 providers, $199/mo for 5‑20, and enterprise custom pricing above that. A 30‑day free trial is offered.
traction: We ran a 3‑month pilot with three independent practices (≈5,000 completed surveys, average NPS 45) and have a waiting list of two more clinics. The pilot shows improved follow‑up rates, but churn risk is still high as we face many established competitors.
team: Founder & CEO Maya Patel was a product manager at a major EHR vendor; CTO Alex Gomez is a former software engineer at a telehealth startup. The core team is just the two of us plus a part‑time UX contractor.
problem: Ambulatory clinics rely on stale paper or email surveys that are delayed, low‑response, and hard to turn into concrete actions, hurting patient experience scores and reimbursement.
solution: PulseLoop delivers a tablet‑based, one‑click survey that triggers automatically at checkout, feeds the data directly into the clinic’s EHR, and surfaces trends and alerts on a simple dashboard so staff can act within the same day.

name: RestEasy Labs
description: We’ve built a compact, FDA‑cleared home sleep apnea testing kit that streams overnight data directly to a cloud platform for AI‑driven analysis. The device costs less than a night in a sleep lab and gives users a clear report and next‑step recommendations within 24 hours.
target_customer: Primary payers are health‑insurers and employer wellness programs that want a cheaper, scalable alternative to in‑clinic polysomnography, while end‑users (patients) benefit from the convenience of testing at home.
pricing: We’re still finalizing the model – initially we’re piloting a per‑test price of $149‑$199, with a discount tier for bulk insurer contracts and a subscription option for employers at roughly $30 per employee per year. The exact structure will evolve as we gather usage data.
